Search for the book in Primo, click “Place Hold,” and sign in [3][4]. Then choose Oxford Campus / King Library as the pickup location, and wait for the notification that it is ready for pickup [2].

No. On the Oxford campus, 3D printing is available at King Library in the MakerSpace [1]. Middletown also has 3D printing in its TEC Lab MakerSpace [2].

Special Collections research access is by appointment only [1][2][3]. To arrange an appointment, use the Special Collections page [2][3]: https://spec.lib.miamioh.edu/home/

Miami University Libraries’ Digital Collections include thousands of digital objects—such as photographs, advertising tradecards, newspapers, manuscripts, and videos—from the Walter Havighurst Special Collections & University Archives [1][7]. You can browse the full set of collections here: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/digital-collections/ [1]. Examples of named collections include the Postcard Collection, Online Civil War Diaries, Cradle of Coaches, KAOS Student Newspaper, and the Freedom Summer Digital Archive [2][9].

Yes — Miami University Libraries do have access to the New York Times for Miami-affiliated users [2]. The databases page also points to a New York Times access link and the newspapers page for access details [1][2].

Miami University faculty/staff download Adobe differently than students; follow the instructions on the Libraries’ Software Checkout page for the faculty/staff option [1][3]. After you check out an “Adobe Acrobat Pro (Faculty/Staff)” license, you can log in at adobe.com using your Miami credentials and download Adobe Acrobat Pro to your own device [5].
Yes—there is a vinyl cutter at the MakerSpace; the MakerSpace equipment page lists it, and you can check the current equipment details there [1].

Acrobat Pro is available through the Library’s Software Checkout/Adobe Creative Cloud page for Oxford [1]. The page says you can reserve Adobe Acrobat Pro and then log in at adobe.com with your Miami credentials after checkout [4].

Yes. You can schedule an appointment by finding and contacting your subject liaison librarian through the Liaisons page: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/about/organization/liaisons/ [1].
Yes — King study rooms are bookable through the room reservations page, and availability is live today [1]. Use the King reservations link to check and book a room [1].
Yes, color printing is available. For the current instructions and details, use the Printing & WiFi page [1].
You can scan documents at the multipurpose printers, and the Printing & WiFi page has the current details [1][2].

The sources point to the Oxford library food/drink policy page for the current rules on bringing food into the library [1][2][3]. One Oxford library event announcement also shows food can be present in King Library during a special program, but that does not state the general policy [4].

Yes — charger cables are available to borrow at the Oxford libraries, including chargers for Mac/PC/phones and other peripherals [1]. The tech checkout page also says equipment can be borrowed at a library checkout desk with your university ID [2].

Yes — Oxford Library data services offers consultations and GIS help, and library staff are happy to help with GIS projects [1][3][4]. The data services page also mentions data visualization support [1][4].

Yes — use the Ask Us page for online chat with a librarian [1][3]. It also offers email, appointment, and phone help for research questions [3][5].
Yes — printing is available at the Oxford libraries, and the operator-verified notes indicate all three have printing [1][2][5]. For current printing details, see the Oxford Printing & WiFi page [3].

For help with your dissertation literature review, use the Miami University Libraries “Ask Us” page to connect with a librarian via chat/email/phone or to set up an appointment/research consultation [1][5]. If you want to work with a subject specialist, use the Subject Liaison (librarian) directory to find the best contact for your discipline [3][4].
